petitioner_background: The petitioner is a strategic port planner with more than 25 years of experience in maritime and civil infrastructure development. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under INA section 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A) via Form I-140. The Nebraska Service Center Director denied the petition, and the AAO dismissed his subsequent appeal, finding he met only two of the ten regulatory criteria. The matter came before the AAO on a combined motion to reopen and reconsider.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: In the prior appeal, the petitioner relied on his membership in the World Association for Waterborne Transport Infrastructure (PIANC) and his position on the board of the Australian Logistics Council (ALC). On motion, he argued for the first time that he meets this criterion based on being 'recently appointed as an Independent Director on the Board of Directors of a distinguished company.' He submitted an August 2018 email from the group managing director confirming the petitioner's intent to apply for the Independent Director position, and documents regarding board meetings in 2019 that the petitioner attended as a director of the company.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the petitioner did not contend it erred in its determination concerning PIANC or ALC (that he had not established those memberships require outstanding achievements as judged by recognized national or international experts), nor did he introduce new evidence relevant to that determination. Regarding the new Independent Director evidence, the AAO found it postdates the filing of the petition in November 2017 — the email was dated August 2018 and board meetings were in 2019. Citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12), Matter of Katigbak, and Matter of Izummi, the AAO held that eligibility must be established from the time of filing and that USCIS cannot consider facts that come into being only subsequent to the filing of a petition. The AAO therefore would not consider this new evidence on motion and found the petitioner had not established he meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a new reference letter from an architect and engineer who worked with him on design projects including a marina in 2006. The letter asserted that the petitioner's 'master's degree research into advanced port planning techniques ... have helped set processes and standards in port planning internationally,' referenced his work in the development of a Future Study which 'has set the future of sea based trade infrastructure for the next 100 years,' and his development of 'theoretical approaches and models to the assessment of all kinds of ports and related infrastructure' and 'cutting-edge techniques in broader infrastructure planning and design.' He also resubmitted copies of previously submitted reference letters. Additionally, he argued for the first time that he meets this criterion based on consulting work on additional projects, including a Container Terminal Sale, an Independent Commercial Review, a Shipping and Capacity Study, and a Terminals Sale, submitting several articles about those projects. He also submitted 'extraordinary ability background information' on the role of ports, the need to modernize U.S. Seaports, and the impact of port infrastructure on economic growth, including articles from the Journal of Shipping and Trade, and the websites www.issues.org, www.aapa-ports.org, www.oecd.org, and www.epa.gov, along with additional articles relating to various projects he was involved in, including a Port Future Study, a port planning study for a Port Authority, and consulting to a Port with a Ports Corporation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the new reference letter demonstrated the petitioner worked on high profile infrastructure planning projects, but did not demonstrate specifically what his original contributions are and how they amount to contributions of major significance. The AAO reiterated that reference letters not providing specifics regarding the petitioner's contributions and their impact on others in the field are insufficient. Regarding the resubmitted reference letters, the AAO cited Matter of O-S-G-, 24 I&N Dec. 56 (BIA 2006), noting that disagreeing with conclusions without establishing legal error is not a ground to reconsider, and declined to re-address previously reviewed letters. Regarding the consulting work on additional projects, the AAO found this evidence postdates the filing of the November 2017 petition — work began in 2018 and 2019 — and cited 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12), Matter of Katigbak, and Matter of Izummi to refuse consideration. The AAO further noted that the articles about those projects do not mention or otherwise refer to the petitioner. Regarding the background information articles about ports generally, the AAO found the petitioner did not explain how those articles establish original contributions of major significance, the articles do not mention the petitioner, and the record does not otherwise document specific contributions of major significance attributable to his work.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed for the first time on motion that he meets this criterion based on his master's thesis, submitting documentation indicating the thesis has been digitally archived at a university library but is 'not available from this repository.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that because the petitioner did not previously claim to have satisfied the scholarly articles criterion, the AAO did not err by failing to consider it. A post-appellate motion to reopen is not the proper forum to advance new claims of eligibility not advanced at any prior stage, and a petitioner abandons issues not raised on appeal, citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226, 1228 n.2 (11th Cir. 2005) and Hristov v. Roark, No. 09-CV-27312011, 2011 WL 4711885 (E.D.N.Y. Sept. 30, 2011). Notwithstanding, the AAO noted the documentation indicating the thesis was digitally archived but 'not available from this repository' does not establish that the thesis was published in professional or major trade publications or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence previously accepted in the prior appellate decision; not re-analyzed on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted in its prior appellate decision that the petitioner had satisfied the leading or critical role criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii). This determination was not revisited on motion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence previously accepted in the prior appellate decision; not re-analyzed on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted in its prior appellate decision that the petitioner had satisfied the high salary criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ix). This determination was not revisited on motion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed both the motion to reconsider and the motion to reopen. The assertions made by the petitioner on motion did not establish that the previous decision was grounded in an incorrect application of law or policy, and the new evidence submitted on motion did not overcome the grounds underlying the previous decision or demonstrate eligibility. The petitioner continued to meet only two of the required three criteria (leading role and high salary), and new evidence relating to membership and original contributions postdated the November 2017 filing and therefore could not be considered. The AAO also noted as a procedural matter that the petitioner failed to include the required statement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii)(C) about whether the unfavorable decision was the subject of any judicial proceeding, which alone is grounds for dismissal under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(4).
legal_citations: Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner failed to include the required statement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii)(C) about whether the unfavorable decision had been or was the subject of any judicial proceeding. The AAO noted that a motion not meeting applicable requirements shall be dismissed under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(4). Additionally, the AAO found procedural issues with the petitioner raising new claims (scholarly articles criterion) for the first time on a post-appellate motion, noting that issues not raised on appeal are deemed abandoned. The AAO also rejected resubmitted reference letters under Matter of O-S-G-, finding the petitioner was improperly using the motion to reconsider to re-argue the same evidence without establishing legal error.
